speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Before we do deeper GitHub work, we need a mental map. If GitHub has ever felt like a maze of tabs, sidebars, and pages that all blur together, this lesson is here to fix that.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And this is especially important for learners using screen readers, magnification, zoom, high contrast themes, or just anyone who wants a reliable way to know, where am I, and what kind of page is this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And one quick administrative note before we begin: this lesson pairs with an audio episode and with two appendices, a glossary and a screen reader cheat sheet. It also points learners to GitHub's own docs and GitHub accessibility documentation as the authoritative references.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is not just theory. It's the orientation system for everything that comes next.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Read or listen to this before navigating GitHub for the first time, because once you understand page structure, landmarks, headings, and URL patterns, you can orient yourself on pages this course never explicitly teaches.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there was also a course-planning note here, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The chapter connects directly to a challenge called Find Your Way Around, which uses ideas from this lesson plus the next repository navigation lessons. And there's a capstone connection too: when you later build AI agents, those agents work through GitHub objects like issues, pull requests, and labels. So learning the manual workflow now is also learning how to collaborate with automation later.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's start with the big picture. GitHub is not one page. It's three nested levels.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I love that already, because many beginners think GitHub is just, quote, the repo page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, but the repo is only one level. Level one is your own account area: your profile, settings, notifications. Think URLs like github dot com slash your username, or slash settings, or slash notifications.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So that's personal space.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Level two is an organization or another user's space. That might be github dot com slash microsoft, or slash community-access, or slash github. That's the public home area for a person or organization.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And level three is the repository.

speaker: ALEX
text: The place where most actual project work happens. Code, issues, pull requests, actions, discussions around changes. A URL like github dot com slash owner slash repo. Or that same path plus slash issues, or slash pull slash 42.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So when someone says, go to the repo, they mean level three.

speaker: ALEX
text: Almost always. And here's a very useful orientation tip for screen reader users: jump to the first level-one heading. On a repository page, the H1 is usually owner slash repo name. On a profile page, it's the username. On a settings page, it's the settings category. The first heading tells you the level.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's talk about what stays consistent, because that's calming.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. No matter where you are on GitHub, there is a global navigation bar at the top. It is your fixed anchor point.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Even if the visual layout shifts around?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and that's an important teaching principle in this chapter: don't over-trust position words like top-right, left side, first, below. GitHub changes layout density, feature rollout, and wrapping over time, and the page can look different depending on screen width or account state.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So instead of saying, click the thing in the top-right, we should anchor to stable identifiers.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Use URL pattern, page title, H1 heading, landmark name, tab name, keyboard shortcut, command name, or exact button text. Those are much more dependable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's in the global navigation area?

speaker: ALEX
text: Commonly: the GitHub logo or home link, global search, a Copilot entry point, links to pull requests and issues across GitHub, notifications, and the profile avatar menu.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for a screen reader user, the key point is not just what the controls are, but where they live.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. They live in the landmark labeled Navigation Menu. If you press D to cycle landmarks, you'll hear Navigation Menu. Once you're there, K moves through links inside that landmark.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And those links are just regular links, not special controls that require forms mode or focus mode.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. That's explicitly called out. In browse mode, press Enter on the links and they work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we give the shortcut highlights too?

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely. Search is often reachable with S or slash. Notifications can be reached with G then N. And if you're moving by landmarks and links, D to Navigation Menu, then K through the available links is a reliable pattern.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What changes once you're inside a repository?

speaker: ALEX
text: You get a second navigation bar below the global one: repository navigation. That's where tabs like Code, Issues, Pull requests, Actions, Projects, Wiki, Security, Insights, and Settings appear.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that landmark has a name too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes: Repository navigation. So on repo pages, you often have two crucial navigation landmarks to recognize: Navigation Menu for GitHub-wide controls, and Repository navigation for this specific project.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The learning cards in this section also addressed different users.

speaker: ALEX
text: They did. For screen reader users: Navigation Menu is the fixed anchor on every page, G then N jumps to notifications, and search results load into Main after using search. For low vision users: the top navigation stays pinned, the notification bell may show an unread dot or badge, and repository tabs show the active tab with a colored underline, which may be easier to perceive in a high contrast theme. For sighted users: remember the visual pattern of a global top bar and then repository tabs below it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, if I'm dropped onto some random GitHub page, how do I tell where I am without guessing?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use three signals: the URL, the browser tab title, and the first H1 heading.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's do the URL first.

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub URLs are wonderfully descriptive. Plain github dot com is your home or personal feed. Github dot com slash username is a user profile. Github dot com slash org is an organization profile. Github dot com slash owner slash repo is the repository home, usually the Code tab.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then adding path pieces tells the story.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Slash issues means the issues list. Slash issues slash 42 means issue number 42. Slash pull slash 7 means pull request 7. Slash tree slash main slash docs means a folder view. Slash blob slash main slash README dot md means a file view. Slash commit slash some hash means a commit page. Slash settings slash accessibility is your accessibility settings. Slash notifications is your notifications inbox.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone uses a screen reader and wants to verify that quickly?

speaker: ALEX
text: Go to the browser address bar. Alt+D on Windows or Command+L on Mac. Listen to the URL. That alone often tells you where you are.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Second signal: browser tab title.

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub titles are also patterned. Repository home might be owner slash repo, then a short description, then GitHub. Issues list is Issues dot owner slash repo. A specific issue includes the issue title, then Issue number, then owner slash repo. A pull request includes the PR title, then Pull Request number, then owner slash repo. Notifications says Notifications dash GitHub. Settings pages often say category dash Settings.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the third signal is the first H1.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. In browse mode, press 1 to jump to the first H1. If you hear owner slash repo, you're on repository home. If you hear Issues, you're on the issues list. If you hear an issue title, you're on an issue detail page. If you hear a PR title, you're on a pull request page. If you hear your username, you're on your profile. If you hear a settings category, you're in settings.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a very teachable routine: URL, title, H1.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Three independent signals. If all three agree, you can trust your orientation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now we get into the page types people actually use all the time.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The lesson identifies five key page types. If you can recognize these, GitHub becomes much less mysterious.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Page type one: repository home on the Code tab.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the central hub of a project. Expect the H1 to be owner slash repo-name. Expect the Repository navigation tabs. Expect a file tree, usually presented as a table. Screen reader users may navigate to the next table and then use table navigation commands such as Control+Alt with arrow keys, depending on screen reader and browser. Below the file tree, you'll often find the rendered README.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And usually some sidebar information too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Commonly an About panel, metadata, release or package info, and project-related side content. The exact arrangement can vary by viewport, but the concepts stay the same: project identity, tabs, file list, README, related metadata.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Page type two is the issues list.

speaker: ALEX
text: The issues list is not one issue. It's the catalog of issues for a repository. Expect the H1 to be Issues. Expect filters and search controls. Expect a list or table-like collection of issue rows, often showing issue title, issue number, labels, author, and update metadata.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this page answers, what open or closed issues exist in this repo?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. It's the scanning page.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Page type three is issue detail.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now you're inside one specific issue. The H1 is the issue title. Nearby you'll usually find the issue number and state, such as open or closed. The main content area typically contains the original issue description and a threaded timeline of comments and events.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then a sidebar with metadata.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Things like assignees, labels, projects, milestones, participants, or linked development information. The key idea is that issue detail is a conversation page around one tracked piece of work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Page type four is pull request detail.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another conversation page, but specifically about proposed code changes. The H1 is the pull request title. You'll encounter tabs like Conversation, Commits, Checks, and Files changed. The Conversation tab looks somewhat like an issue thread, but it includes review events, approval states, requested changes, CI checks, merge status, and code-related discussion.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the Files Changed tab is a distinct mode.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very much so. That's where you inspect diffs. It has its own structure and is often denser, especially for screen reader users.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then page type five combines your personal feed and your profile.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Your feed, at github dot com, is your activity hub: updates from repositories and people you follow, recent work, notifications of interest, suggested activity. Your profile, at github dot com slash your username, is your identity page: your name or username, bio, pinned repositories, contribution activity, repositories tab, and public-facing account information.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So feed is what is happening around you, and profile is who you are on GitHub.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's a nice concise distinction.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Were there learning card takeaways for this whole section?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes: train yourself to classify a page quickly. Is it a repo home, issues list, issue detail, PR detail, feed, or profile? Once you know the type, you can predict landmarks, headings, controls, and what task is possible there.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's build a more concrete mental image of a repository page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Picture a repository home page as layers. First, the global Navigation Menu at the top. Then the Repository navigation bar with tabs like Code and Issues. Then the main content area with repository identity and controls. After that, the file tree listing folders and files. Then the rendered README below. And often alongside or after the main stream, a sidebar with About and other metadata.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So for a sighted learner, this is a visual stack. For a screen reader user, it becomes a landmark and heading sequence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And the lesson explicitly includes screen reader navigation of this type of page. A practical pattern is: jump by landmarks first, confirm the H1, move to Repository navigation if you need tabs, move into Main, then use headings, tables, and links to inspect the file list and README.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So don't just arrow from the top of the page and hope.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Use structure intentionally.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That leads perfectly into the screen reader orientation sequence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. This is one of the most important practical parts of the lesson. When you land on a GitHub page, use a consistent sequence.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Walk us through it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Step one: identify the page using URL, tab title, or H1. Step two: jump through landmarks with D so you know the major regions available. Step three: listen for Navigation Menu and, if you're inside a repository, Repository navigation. Step four: jump to the H1 with 1 and confirm the page type. Step five: move into Main and use headings, links, buttons, tables, and lists based on the page's purpose.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So instead of, where do I start, you always start with the same ritual.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That's the point of the learning cards here: orientation should become procedural. Land on page. Check URL. Check title. Check H1. Scan landmarks. Then act.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That kind of repeatable routine lowers cognitive load.

speaker: ALEX
text: A lot.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's get even more specific: landmark structure by page type.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Different GitHub pages share some landmarks, but each page type tends to have a recognizable pattern.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Start with repository home.

speaker: ALEX
text: Repository home often includes the Navigation Menu landmark first, then Repository navigation, then Main. Within Main, you may encounter search or filtering controls, a table for the file tree, regions related to README content, and complementary or aside-style information depending on browser and accessibility tree exposure.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Issues list page?

speaker: ALEX
text: Again, Navigation Menu, then Repository navigation, then Main. Inside Main, expect filtering controls, issue list content, and possibly pagination or additional controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Issue detail?

speaker: ALEX
text: Navigation Menu, Repository navigation, Main. But Main now contains a page heading for the issue title, the issue metadata, the conversation timeline, comment composer, and sidebar metadata such as labels or assignees.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Pull request conversation tab?

speaker: ALEX
text: Similar to issue detail in landmark flow, but Main includes pull request status information, branch and merge context, review conversation, checks, and related controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And Files Changed?

speaker: ALEX
text: Still under the same broader shell, but Main becomes diff-centric. Expect file-by-file changed sections, controls for viewing or filtering diffs, and a much more granular navigation experience. This is where headings and quick navigation between file sections become especially helpful.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the lesson isn't promising that landmarks are identical forever, but it is teaching a durable expectation pattern.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The stable lesson is the order of orientation: global shell, repo shell if applicable, then page-specific main content.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pair that with heading hierarchy, because landmarks get you to regions, but headings tell you the internal outline.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said. On repository home, the H1 is owner slash repo. Lower-level headings may introduce content sections like README areas, releases, or side panels, depending on page structure.

speaker: JAMIE
text: On the issues list, the H1 is simply Issues.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and subheadings may group content or support filtering and list organization.

speaker: JAMIE
text: On issue detail, the H1 is the issue title.

speaker: ALEX
text: Then lower headings can identify conversation blocks, side sections, or related metadata areas.

speaker: JAMIE
text: For pull request detail on the Conversation tab, the H1 is the PR title.

speaker: ALEX
text: And below that, headings may separate status, timeline content, reviews, checks summaries, and related sections.

speaker: JAMIE
text: While on Files Changed, the heading structure often helps you hop between changed files.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Files Changed tends to reward heading navigation because each file section can act like a chunk you want to inspect independently.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learning card takeaway is to not just use heading navigation randomly. Use it with a prediction based on page type.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Know what H1 should be, then use the rest of the heading tree as your internal map of the page.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's talk about viewport, because GitHub can feel like a moving target when you zoom or shrink the browser.

speaker: ALEX
text: And that's normal. At full desktop width, roughly twelve hundred pixels and up, GitHub often presents side-by-side layouts. Sidebars are more likely to stay alongside main content. Navigation elements may all appear expanded.

speaker: JAMIE
text: At tablet widths?

speaker: ALEX
text: Roughly seven hundred sixty-eight to just under twelve hundred pixels, elements may wrap. Sidebars may shift lower. Controls may compress. The same information is still present, but not necessarily in the same visual arrangement.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And on mobile widths below that?

speaker: ALEX
text: Layouts become more stacked and condensed. Menus may collapse. Side content often moves below main content. Some control labels may shorten or become icon-triggered. This matters for both mobile devices and desktop users who zoom heavily.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So again, don't anchor too strongly to spatial instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. This is why the chapter insists on durable cues: names, landmarks, headings, URLs, buttons, tabs, and shortcuts. Those survive viewport shifts better than screen position descriptions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now for the mental model itself. If you wanted a learner to carry one internal picture away from this lesson, what is it?

speaker: ALEX
text: I would say this: every GitHub page is a shell plus a page type. The shell is the global navigation, and sometimes repository navigation. The page type is the specific work surface: repo home, issues list, issue detail, PR detail, profile, feed, and so on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So first identify the shell, then identify the page type.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Then predict what should be there. On repo home, expect files and README. On issues list, expect filters and issue rows. On issue detail, expect a conversation thread plus metadata. On PR detail, expect review and changed files. On feed, expect activity. On profile, identity and repositories.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That predictive ability is what makes a site stop feeling random.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The learning cards call this building your internal map. You're not memorizing every page. You're learning how to infer structure from a few reliable signals.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The lesson also includes a practice activity: the 60-second orientation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Here's the exercise. Open any GitHub page and give yourself one minute. First, identify the URL pattern. Second, listen to or inspect the browser tab title. Third, jump to the H1. Fourth, cycle landmarks. Fifth, state out loud what page type you're on and what you expect to find next.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a great habit.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. You can do it on a repository home, an issues list, an issue detail page, a pull request, your notifications, or your profile. The goal is not speed for its own sake. The goal is certainty.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone gets stuck?

speaker: ALEX
text: Return to the three signals. Check the URL. Check the tab title. Check the first H1. Then jump by landmarks. If needed, go back to a simpler known page like the repository Code tab or your notifications page. Use the glossary. Use the screen reader cheat sheet. And when GitHub's layout feels different than expected, trust labels and landmark names more than visual position.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's such an important accessibility reminder: the page may have changed shape, but it usually hasn't changed identity.

speaker: ALEX
text: Beautifully put.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There was also a short Day 2 amplifier in the chapter.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The idea is that this orientation skill becomes even more powerful the next day when you start working with automation and AI agents. Agents operate through the same objects you now understand: issues, pull requests, labels, repository pages. So a solid mental model of GitHub's structure is foundational, not optional.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we close, can you give a compact recap?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. GitHub has three levels: personal account, organization or user space, and repository. Every page has a global Navigation Menu. Repository pages also have Repository navigation. You can orient using URL, browser tab title, and the first H1. Learn the five key page types: repository home, issues list, issue detail, pull request detail, and your feed or profile. Use landmarks first, then headings. Expect layouts to change across desktop, tablet, mobile, and high zoom. Build an internal map so you can predict what belongs on each page.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if you're using a screen reader, make your routine automatic: address bar if needed, 1 for H1, D for landmarks, K for links within navigation, and stable labels over visual guesswork.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Once you stop treating GitHub as a giant undifferentiated website and start recognizing its page types and structure, it becomes teachable, navigable, and much less stressful.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's the goal. We'll build on that from here.
